title: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: Pathophysiology and Management
abstract: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is a chronic metabolic disorder characterized by hyperglycemia resulting from insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. The global prevalence of T2DM has risen dramatically over the past three decades, affecting approximately 462 million individuals worldwide. Pathophysiology involves impaired insulin signaling in peripheral tissues, particularly skeletal muscle, liver, and adipose tissue. First-line pharmacotherapy includes metformin, which reduces hepatic glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity. Lifestyle modifications including dietary changes, regular physical activity, and weight management remain cornerstone interventions. Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) targets of less than 7% are recommended for most adults. Complications include diabetic nephropathy, retinopathy, neuropathy, and increased cardiovascular risk. Regular screening for microvascular and macrovascular complications is essential for comprehensive diabetes care.

title: Hypertension: Current Evidence and Clinical Practice Guidelines
abstract: Hypertension affects approximately 1.28 billion adults globally and is the leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease, stroke, and chronic kidney disease. The 2017 ACC/AHA guidelines define hypertension as systolic blood pressure ≥130 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure ≥80 mmHg. Primary hypertension accounts for 90-95% of cases and is influenced by genetic, environmental, and lifestyle factors including sodium intake, obesity, physical inactivity, and excessive alcohol consumption. First-line antihypertensive agents include thiazide diuretics, ACE inhibitors, angiotensin receptor blockers (ARBs), and calcium channel blockers. Treatment targets vary by patient population: <130/80 mmHg for high cardiovascular risk patients and <140/90 mmHg for lower risk populations. Home blood pressure monitoring and ambulatory blood pressure monitoring improve diagnostic accuracy and treatment titration.

title: Asthma: Diagnosis, Classification, and Stepwise Management
abstract: Asthma is a chronic inflammatory airway disease affecting over 300 million people worldwide, characterized by variable airflow obstruction, bronchial hyperresponsiveness, and airway remodeling. Diagnosis relies on clinical history of episodic wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, and cough, confirmed by spirometry demonstrating reversible airflow limitation (≥12% and 200 mL improvement in FEV1 post-bronchodilator). Classification ranges from intermittent to severe persistent based on symptom frequency, nighttime awakenings, and lung function. The Global Initiative for Asthma (GINA) recommends stepwise pharmacotherapy: Step 1 involves as-needed low-dose inhaled corticosteroid (ICS)-formoterol; Steps 2-5 involve escalating ICS doses with long-acting beta-agonists (LABAs), leukotriene receptor antagonists, and biologic therapies for severe, uncontrolled asthma. Trigger avoidance, patient education, and written asthma action plans are critical components of comprehensive asthma management.

title: Coronary Artery Disease: Risk Factors, Diagnosis, and Interventions
abstract: Coronary artery disease (CAD) remains the leading cause of death worldwide, responsible for approximately 9 million deaths annually. Atherosclerosis, the underlying pathology, involves progressive lipid accumulation, inflammation, and plaque formation within coronary arteries. Major risk factors include dyslipidemia, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, smoking, obesity, family history, and sedentary lifestyle. The Framingham Risk Score and ASCVD pooled cohort equations are used for cardiovascular risk assessment. Diagnosis involves stress testing (exercise or pharmacologic), coronary CT angiography, and invasive coronary angiography. Medical management includes antiplatelet therapy (aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitors), statins, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors/ARBs. Revascularization options include percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) with stent placement and coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG) for multivessel or left main disease. Cardiac rehabilitation programs improve outcomes and reduce mortality by 20-25%.

title: Major Depressive Disorder: Neurobiology and Treatment Approaches
abstract: Major depressive disorder (MDD) is a prevalent psychiatric condition affecting approximately 280 million people globally, characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, cognitive impairment, and neurovegetative symptoms lasting at least two weeks. The monoamine hypothesis implicates dysregulation of serotonin, norepinephrine, and dopamine neurotransmitter systems. Modern understanding also involves neuroinflammation, HPA axis dysregulation, neuroplasticity deficits, and gut-brain axis interactions. First-line pharmacotherapy includes selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) such as sertraline, fluoxetine, and escitalopram. Serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (SNRIs) like venlafaxine and duloxetine are alternatives. Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) demonstrates equal efficacy to pharmacotherapy for mild-to-moderate depression. Combination therapy (medication + psychotherapy) shows superior outcomes for moderate-to-severe MDD. Treatment-resistant depression may benefit from ketamine/esketamine, transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), or electroconvulsive therapy (ECT).

title: Community-Acquired Pneumonia: Etiology, Diagnosis, and Antibiotic Management
abstract: Community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) is a leading infectious cause of hospitalization and death, with an annual incidence of 5-11 per 1,000 adults. Streptococcus pneumoniae remains the most common bacterial pathogen, followed by Haemophilus influenzae, Mycoplasma pneumoniae, and respiratory viruses (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, RSV). Clinical presentation includes cough, fever, dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, and focal consolidation on chest radiography. Severity assessment tools include CURB-65 and the Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI) to guide disposition decisions. Outpatient treatment for low-risk patients includes amoxicillin or doxycycline; moderate-risk patients require respiratory fluoroquinolones or beta-lactam plus macrolide combinations. Hospitalized patients receive intravenous beta-lactam plus macrolide therapy or respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy. Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination are key preventive strategies.

title: Chronic Kidney Disease: Staging, Progression, and Renoprotective Strategies
abstract: Chronic kidney disease (CKD) affects approximately 850 million people worldwide and is classified into five stages based on glomerular filtration rate (GFR): Stage 1 (GFR ≥90), Stage 2 (60-89), Stage 3a (45-59), Stage 3b (30-44), Stage 4 (15-29), and Stage 5 (<15 mL/min/1.73m²). Leading causes include diabetes mellitus (40%), hypertension (28%), and glomerulonephritis (10%). Albuminuria (>30 mg/g creatinine) is an early marker of kidney damage and independently predicts cardiovascular risk. Renoprotective strategies include RAAS inhibition with ACE inhibitors or ARBs, SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) which reduce CKD progression by 30-40%, blood pressure control (<130/80 mmHg), glycemic optimization, dietary protein restriction, and avoidance of nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast dye). Referral to nephrology is recommended at Stage 4 or earlier if rapidly declining GFR.

title: Breast Cancer Screening, Diagnosis, and Multimodal Treatment
abstract: Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy in women worldwide, with approximately 2.3 million new cases annually. Mammographic screening reduces breast cancer mortality by 20-30% in women aged 50-74 years. The American Cancer Society recommends annual mammography beginning at age 45, transitioning to biennial screening at age 55. Diagnostic workup includes diagnostic mammography, breast ultrasound, and MRI for high-risk patients, with tissue diagnosis via core needle biopsy. Molecular subtyping (Luminal A, Luminal B, HER2-enriched, triple-negative) guides treatment selection. Treatment modalities include surgery (lumpectomy vs mastectomy), radiation therapy, chemotherapy (anthracycline and taxane-based regimens), endocrine therapy (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors) for hormone receptor-positive disease, and targeted therapy (trastuzumab for HER2-positive disease). Immunotherapy with checkpoint inhibitors shows promise for triple-negative breast cancer.

title: Alzheimer's Disease: Pathogenesis, Biomarkers, and Emerging Therapies
abstract: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is the most common cause of dementia, affecting approximately 55 million people globally. Neuropathological hallmarks include extracellular amyloid-beta (Aβ) plaques and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles composed of hyperphosphorylated tau protein. The amyloid cascade hypothesis posits that Aβ accumulation triggers synaptic dysfunction, neuroinflammation, and neuronal death. Cerebrospinal fluid biomarkers (Aβ42, phospho-tau, total tau) and amyloid/tau PET imaging enable early diagnosis. Clinical stages progress from preclinical AD to mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to dementia. Current symptomatic treatments include cholinesterase inhibitors (donepezil, rivastigmine, galantamine) and the NMDA receptor antagonist memantine. Disease-modifying therapies targeting amyloid (lecanemab, donanemab) have demonstrated modest clinical benefit in early-stage AD. Non-pharmacological interventions including cognitive stimulation, physical exercise, social engagement, and cardiovascular risk factor management may slow cognitive decline.

title: Antibiotic Resistance: Mechanisms, Surveillance, and Stewardship
abstract: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is recognized by the WHO as one of the top global public health threats, responsible for approximately 1.27 million deaths directly attributable to resistant infections annually. Key resistance mechanisms include enzymatic drug inactivation (beta-lactamases, aminoglycoside-modifying enzymes), target site modification (altered penicillin-binding proteins, ribosomal mutations), decreased permeability (porin mutations), and active efflux pumps. Priority pathogens include carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae (CRE), methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA), vancomycin-resistant Enterococci (VRE), and extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing organisms. Antimicrobial stewardship programs (ASPs) reduce inappropriate antibiotic use by 20-30% through strategies including prospective audit and feedback, formulary restrictions, antibiotic time-outs, and de-escalation protocols. Rapid diagnostic technologies (PCR panels, MALDI-TOF) improve pathogen identification and targeted therapy selection.

title: Obesity: Pathophysiology, Comorbidities, and Multimodal Interventions
abstract: Obesity, defined as BMI ≥30 kg/m², affects over 650 million adults globally and is a major risk factor for type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, osteoarthritis, and obstructive sleep apnea. The pathophysiology involves complex interactions between genetic predisposition, neuroendocrine regulation of appetite (leptin, ghrelin, GLP-1), energy expenditure, and environmental influences. Adipose tissue dysfunction leads to chronic low-grade inflammation, insulin resistance, and metabolic syndrome. Evidence-based interventions include lifestyle modification (caloric restriction, increased physical activity, behavioral counseling) as first-line therapy. Pharmacotherapy options include GLP-1 receptor agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide), which achieve 10-15% weight loss, phentermine-topiramate, and naltrexone-bupropion. Bariatric surgery (Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy) produces durable weight loss of 25-35% and significant comorbidity improvement, with mortality benefit demonstrated in long-term follow-up studies.

title: Stroke: Acute Management and Secondary Prevention
abstract: Stroke is the second leading cause of death and a major cause of long-term disability globally. Ischemic stroke accounts for 87% of cases and results from arterial occlusion due to thrombosis or embolism. The remaining 13% are hemorrhagic strokes (intracerebral or subarachnoid hemorrhage). Acute ischemic stroke management centers on rapid reperfusion: intravenous alteplase (tPA) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset and mechanical thrombectomy for large vessel occlusion within 24 hours in selected patients. The NIHSS score quantifies neurological deficit severity. Secondary prevention strategies include antiplatelet therapy (aspirin, clopidogrel), anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation (DOACs preferred over warfarin), statin therapy, blood pressure management, carotid endarterectomy or stenting for significant carotid stenosis, and lifestyle modifications. Stroke rehabilitation involving physical, occupational, and speech therapy is essential for functional recovery.

title: Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease: Diagnosis and GOLD-Based Management
abstract: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) affects approximately 380 million people worldwide and is the third leading cause of death globally. The disease is characterized by persistent airflow limitation due to chronic bronchitis and/or emphysema, primarily caused by cigarette smoking (85-90% of cases) and environmental/occupational exposures. Diagnosis requires post-bronchodilator spirometry demonstrating FEV1/FVC ratio <0.70. The GOLD (Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease) classification combines spirometric severity (GOLD 1-4) with symptom burden (mMRC/CAT scores) and exacerbation history to guide therapy. Pharmacotherapy includes short-acting bronchodilators for rescue use, long-acting muscarinic antagonists (LAMAs) and long-acting beta-agonists (LABAs) as maintenance therapy, and inhaled corticosteroids (ICS) for patients with frequent exacerbations and elevated eosinophils. Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow disease progression. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, dyspnea, and quality of life.

title: Hepatitis B and C: Screening, Natural History, and Antiviral Therapy
abstract: Viral hepatitis B (HBV) and C (HCV) infections affect approximately 296 million and 58 million people globally, respectively, and are leading causes of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma. HBV is transmitted through blood, sexual contact, and vertical (mother-to-child) transmission. Chronic HBV is managed with nucleos(t)ide analogues (entecavir, tenofovir) that suppress viral replication but rarely achieve functional cure. Universal HBV vaccination remains the most effective preventive strategy. HCV is primarily transmitted through blood exposure, particularly injection drug use. Direct-acting antivirals (DAAs) including sofosbuvir-velpatasvir and glecaprevir-pibrentasvir achieve sustained virologic response (cure) rates >95% with 8-12 weeks of treatment, representing one of the greatest therapeutic advances in infectious disease. Screening is recommended for all adults aged 18-79 years. Liver fibrosis assessment using FibroScan or serum biomarkers (FIB-4, APRI) guides treatment urgency and surveillance for hepatocellular carcinoma.

title: Thyroid Disorders: Hypothyroidism and Hyperthyroidism Diagnosis and Management
abstract: Thyroid disorders are among the most common endocrine conditions, with hypothyroidism affecting 5-10% and hyperthyroidism affecting 1-2% of the population. Primary hypothyroidism is most commonly caused by Hashimoto's thyroiditis (autoimmune) and is diagnosed by elevated TSH with low free T4. Symptoms include fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, and depression. Treatment with levothyroxine replacement targets TSH within the reference range (0.5-4.5 mIU/L), with dose adjustments every 6-8 weeks. Hyperthyroidism is most commonly caused by Graves' disease (autoimmune), toxic multinodular goiter, or toxic adenoma. Symptoms include weight loss, heat intolerance, palpitations, tremor, and anxiety. Diagnosis shows suppressed TSH with elevated free T4/T3. Treatment options include antithyroid medications (methimazole, preferred over propylthiouracil except in first trimester pregnancy), radioactive iodine ablation, and thyroidectomy. Beta-blockers provide symptomatic relief during the hyperthyroid state.

title: Iron Deficiency Anemia: Diagnosis, Etiology, and Iron Replacement Strategies
abstract: Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide, affecting approximately 1.2 billion people. It is particularly prevalent in premenopausal women, pregnant women, children, and individuals with chronic gastrointestinal blood loss. Laboratory diagnosis requires a complete blood count showing microcytic hypochromic anemia (low MCV, low MCH), along with iron studies demonstrating low serum ferritin (<30 ng/mL), low serum iron, elevated total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), and low transferrin saturation (<20%). Etiologies include inadequate dietary intake, malabsorption (celiac disease, gastric bypass), chronic blood loss (menorrhagia, GI bleeding from ulcers, malignancy, or angiodysplasia), and increased demand (pregnancy, growth). Evaluation for underlying cause is essential, particularly GI evaluation in men and postmenopausal women. Oral iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate 325 mg, providing 65 mg elemental iron) is first-line therapy; alternate-day dosing improves fractional absorption. Intravenous iron (ferric carboxymaltose, iron sucrose) is indicated for oral iron intolerance, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion.

title: COVID-19: Virology, Variants, and Evidence-Based Treatment Protocols
abstract: SARS-CoV-2, the causative agent of COVID-19, is a positive-sense single-stranded RNA betacoronavirus that enters human cells via the ACE2 receptor. Since the initial outbreak in December 2019, the virus has undergone significant evolution through variants of concern (Alpha, Delta, Omicron and sublineages). Clinical presentation ranges from asymptomatic infection to severe acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) with multiorgan failure. Risk factors for severe disease include advanced age, obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, immunosuppression, and chronic lung disease. Treatment advances include antivirals (nirmatrelvir-ritonavir/Paxlovid for early outpatient use, remdesivir for hospitalized patients), immunomodulators (dexamethasone reducing 28-day mortality by one-third in patients requiring oxygen, tocilizumab/baricitinib for severe disease), and anticoagulation for hospitalized patients. mRNA vaccines (BNT162b2, mRNA-1273) and updated boosters targeting circulating variants remain the cornerstone of prevention, reducing severe disease and death by >90%.

title: Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease: Pathophysiology and Stepwise Therapy
abstract: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) affects approximately 20% of Western populations and is characterized by troublesome symptoms or complications from reflux of gastric contents into the esophagus. The primary pathophysiologic mechanism involves transient lower esophageal sphincter (LES) relaxations. Typical symptoms include heartburn and regurgitation; atypical presentations include chronic cough, laryngitis, dental erosions, and chest pain. Diagnosis is primarily clinical for typical symptoms; diagnostic testing (upper endoscopy, ambulatory pH monitoring, high-resolution manometry) is reserved for atypical symptoms, refractory cases, or alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, GI bleeding). Lifestyle modifications include weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoiding late meals, and dietary triggers (caffeine, alcohol, spicy foods). Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are first-line pharmacotherapy, providing superior acid suppression compared to H2 receptor antagonists. Long-term PPI use has been associated with potential risks including Clostridioides difficile infection, hypomagnesemia, and bone fractures, warranting periodic reassessment of need. Anti-reflux surgery (laparoscopic fundoplication) is an option for medically refractory cases.

title: Urinary Tract Infections: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Recurrence Prevention
abstract: Urinary tract infections (UTIs) are among the most common bacterial infections, with 150 million episodes annually worldwide. Women are disproportionately affected, with a lifetime risk of approximately 50%. Escherichia coli accounts for 75-95% of uncomplicated UTIs. Clinical presentation of cystitis includes dysuria, urinary frequency, urgency, and suprapubic discomfort. Pyelonephritis additionally presents with flank pain, fever, and costovertebral angle tenderness. Diagnosis relies on urinalysis (pyuria, bacteriuria) and urine culture (≥10⁵ CFU/mL). Empiric treatment for uncomplicated cystitis includes nitrofurantoin (5 days), trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (3 days), or fosfomycin (single dose), guided by local resistance patterns. Complicated UTIs and pyelonephritis require broader-spectrum antibiotics (fluoroquinolones, third-generation cephalosporins). Recurrent UTIs (≥3 per year or ≥2 in 6 months) may benefit from prophylactic strategies including post-coital prophylaxis, continuous low-dose prophylaxis, cranberry products, vaginal estrogen in postmenopausal women, and behavioral modifications.

title: Rheumatoid Arthritis: Early Diagnosis and Treat-to-Target Strategies
abstract: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) is a chronic systemic autoimmune disease affecting approximately 1% of the global population, characterized by symmetrical inflammatory polyarthritis primarily targeting the small joints of the hands and feet. Early diagnosis using the 2010 ACR/EULAR classification criteria, which incorporate joint involvement, serology (RF, anti-CCP antibodies), acute-phase reactants (ESR, CRP), and symptom duration, enables timely intervention. The treat-to-target approach aims for clinical remission or low disease activity within 6 months. Conventional synthetic DMARDs (csDMARDs), particularly methotrexate, remain the anchor of initial therapy. Biologic DMARDs (TNF inhibitors: adalimumab, etanercept; IL-6 inhibitors: tocilizumab; B-cell depleting agents: rituximab; T-cell co-stimulation modulators: abatacept) are used for inadequate response to csDMARDs. Targeted synthetic DMARDs (JAK inhibitors: tofacitinib, baricitinib) offer oral alternatives to biologics. Extra-articular manifestations include interstitial lung disease, cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and increased infection risk from immunosuppressive therapy.

title: Sepsis and Septic Shock: Early Recognition and Surviving Sepsis Guidelines
abstract: Sepsis is defined as life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection, affecting approximately 49 million people annually with a mortality rate of 20-30%. The Sequential Organ Failure Assessment (SOFA) score identifies organ dysfunction, while the quick SOFA (qSOFA) provides bedside screening (altered mentation, systolic BP ≤100 mmHg, respiratory rate ≥22). Septic shock is sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors and serum lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate volume resuscitation. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SSC) emphasizes early recognition and the 1-hour bundle: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad-spectrum antibiotics, begin rapid fluid resuscitation (30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate ≥4 mmol/L), and apply vasopressors (norepinephrine first-line) for persistent hypotension. Source control, de-escalation of antibiotics based on culture results, and multimodal organ support are critical. Early goal-directed therapy reduces mortality significantly when implemented within the first hours.

title: Osteoporosis: Bone Density Assessment and Fracture Prevention
abstract: Osteoporosis is a systemic skeletal disease characterized by low bone mineral density (BMD) and microarchitectural deterioration, leading to increased fracture susceptibility. It affects approximately 200 million women worldwide and is responsible for over 8.9 million fractures annually. Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) is the gold standard for BMD measurement, with T-scores of -1.0 to -2.5 indicating osteopenia and ≤-2.5 indicating osteoporosis. The FRAX tool estimates 10-year fracture probability incorporating clinical risk factors. Non-pharmacological prevention includes adequate calcium intake (1000-1200 mg/day), vitamin D supplementation (800-1000 IU/day), weight-bearing exercise, and fall prevention. Pharmacotherapy is indicated for fragility fractures, T-score ≤-2.5, or high FRAX risk. First-line agents include oral bisphosphonates (alendronate, risedronate) which reduce vertebral fracture risk by 40-70%. Alternatives include denosumab (RANKL inhibitor), romosozumab (anti-sclerostin, anabolic), and teriparatide (PTH analogue, anabolic) for severe osteoporosis.

title: Atrial Fibrillation: Rhythm Control, Rate Control, and Stroke Prevention
abstract: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common sustained cardiac arrhythmia, affecting approximately 37.5 million people worldwide. It is characterized by irregular, rapid atrial activation leading to an irregularly irregular ventricular response. Major risk factors include age, hypertension, heart failure, valvular heart disease, obesity, obstructive sleep apnea, diabetes, and thyroid disease. The CHA₂DS₂-VASc score stratifies thromboembolic risk: scores ≥2 in men or ≥3 in women warrant oral anticoagulation. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs: apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran) are preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF due to superior safety profiles. Rate control (beta-blockers, non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers, digoxin) targets resting heart rate <110 bpm for lenient or <80 bpm for strict control. Rhythm control strategies include antiarrhythmic drugs (amiodarone, flecainide, sotalol) and catheter ablation (pulmonary vein isolation), which demonstrates superior rhythm control in selected patients. Left atrial appendage occlusion is an alternative for patients with contraindications to anticoagulation.

title: Acute Kidney Injury: Classification, Etiology, and Management Principles
abstract: Acute kidney injury (AKI) is defined by a rapid decline in kidney function, identified by an increase in serum creatinine (≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours or ≥1.5x baseline within 7 days) or decreased urine output (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6 hours). The KDIGO classification stages AKI from 1-3 based on creatinine elevation and urine output criteria. Etiologies are categorized as prerenal (volume depletion, heart failure, sepsis — 60% of cases), intrinsic renal (acute tubular necrosis, interstitial nephritis, glomerulonephritis — 35%), and postrenal (urinary obstruction — 5%). Management principles include identifying and treating the underlying cause, optimizing volume status, avoiding nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast dye), adjusting medication doses for reduced GFR, and monitoring for complications (hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, fluid overload, uremia). Renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis, continuous renal replacement therapy) is indicated for refractory hyperkalemia, severe metabolic acidosis, volume overload, or uremic complications. AKI survivors require follow-up for CKD development.

title: Migraine: Pathophysiology, Acute Treatment, and Preventive Therapies
abstract: Migraine is a neurovascular disorder affecting approximately 15% of the global population, characterized by recurrent episodes of moderate-to-severe unilateral pulsating headache lasting 4-72 hours, accompanied by nausea, photophobia, and phonophobia. Approximately 25% of patients experience aura (visual, sensory, or language disturbances). The trigeminovascular system and calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) play central roles in migraine pathophysiology. Acute treatment includes NSAIDs and acetaminophen for mild attacks, triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan) as first-line specific therapy, and emerging gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant) and ditans (lasmiditan) for patients with cardiovascular contraindications to triptans. Preventive therapy is recommended for ≥4 migraine days per month and includes beta-blockers (propranolol), anticonvulsants (topiramate, valproate), antidepressants (amitriptyline), and CGRP monoclonal antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) which represent a breakthrough in migraine prevention. Lifestyle modifications including regular sleep, hydration, stress management, and trigger avoidance complement pharmacotherapy.

title: Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism: Diagnosis and Anticoagulation
abstract: Venous thromboembolism (VTE), encompassing deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE), affects approximately 1-2 per 1,000 individuals annually. Virchow's triad (venous stasis, endothelial injury, hypercoagulability) remains the conceptual framework for VTE pathogenesis. Risk factors include surgery, immobilization, malignancy, pregnancy, oral contraceptives, thrombophilia, and obesity. DVT typically presents with unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, and erythema; the Wells score guides diagnostic probability assessment. Compression ultrasonography is the diagnostic standard for DVT; CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) is the gold standard for PE diagnosis. D-dimer testing has high negative predictive value for excluding VTE in low-probability patients. Treatment with anticoagulation includes DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) as first-line for most patients, with initial parenteral heparin bridging to warfarin as an alternative. Treatment duration is 3 months for provoked VTE and extended/indefinite for unprovoked or recurrent events. Catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical thrombectomy is reserved for massive PE with hemodynamic instability.

title: Celiac Disease: Serologic Screening, Diagnosis, and Gluten-Free Diet Management
abstract: Celiac disease is an autoimmune disorder triggered by gluten ingestion in genetically susceptible individuals (HLA-DQ2/DQ8), affecting approximately 1% of the global population. Gluten peptides trigger an immune response in the small intestinal mucosa, leading to villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and malabsorption. Clinical manifestations range from classic gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, steatorrhea, abdominal bloating, weight loss) to extraintestinal manifestations (iron deficiency anemia, osteoporosis, dermatitis herpetiformis, neurological symptoms, infertility). Serologic screening with tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA antibodies has >95% sensitivity and specificity. Diagnosis is confirmed by duodenal biopsy demonstrating Marsh grade 2-3 histological changes while on a gluten-containing diet. A strict, lifelong gluten-free diet (GFD) eliminating wheat, barley, and rye is the only established treatment, resulting in clinical improvement within weeks and mucosal healing within 6-24 months. Nutritional monitoring for deficiencies (iron, folate, calcium, vitamin D, B12) and bone density assessment are important components of long-term management.

title: Type 1 Diabetes: Autoimmune Pathogenesis and Insulin Therapy
abstract: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (T1DM) is an autoimmune disease characterized by T-cell mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells, resulting in absolute insulin deficiency. It accounts for 5-10% of all diabetes cases and typically presents in childhood or adolescence, although adult-onset cases (LADA) occur. Autoantibodies (GAD65, IA-2, ZnT8, insulin autoantibodies) are present in >90% of patients at diagnosis and can be detected years before clinical onset. Presentation often includes polyuria, polydipsia, weight loss, and diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) in 30-40% of new diagnoses. Treatment requires lifelong exogenous insulin therapy via multiple daily injections (MDI) using basal-bolus regimens (long-acting insulin glargine/detemir + rapid-acting insulin lispro/aspart) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII) via insulin pump. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) systems and hybrid closed-loop (artificial pancreas) systems significantly improve glycemic control and reduce hypoglycemia. Target HbA1c is <7% for most adults. Carbohydrate counting, hypoglycemia education, and screening for associated autoimmune conditions (thyroid disease, celiac disease) are essential components of care.

title: Heart Failure: Classification, Pharmacotherapy, and Device Therapy
abstract: Heart failure (HF) affects approximately 64 million people worldwide and is classified by ejection fraction: HFrEF (EF ≤40%), HFmrEF (EF 41-49%), and HFpEF (EF ≥50%). Common etiologies include coronary artery disease (65%), hypertension, valvular heart disease, cardiomyopathies, and myocarditis. Symptoms include dyspnea, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, peripheral edema, and fatigue. BNP (>100 pg/mL) or NT-proBNP (>300 pg/mL) aid in diagnosis and prognosis. Guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for HFrEF includes the four-pillar approach: ACE inhibitors/ARBs/ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan), beta-blockers (metoprolol succinate, carvedilol, bisoprolol), mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone), and SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide) manage volume overload but do not improve mortality. Device therapy includes implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death (EF ≤35%) and cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) for patients with EF ≤35% and QRS ≥150 ms. Advanced therapies include left ventricular assist devices (LVADs) and cardiac transplantation for end-stage HF.

title: Preventive Healthcare: Screening Recommendations and Vaccination Schedules
abstract: Preventive healthcare encompasses evidence-based screening, immunization, and counseling interventions designed to detect diseases early or prevent their occurrence. The United States Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) provides graded recommendations (A, B, C, D, I) based on net benefit analysis. Key cancer screenings include: colonoscopy or stool-based testing for colorectal cancer (ages 45-75), low-dose CT for lung cancer in high-risk smokers (ages 50-80, ≥20 pack-year history), mammography for breast cancer (ages 40-74), Pap smear/HPV co-testing for cervical cancer (ages 21-65), and PSA-based screening discussion for prostate cancer (ages 55-69). Cardiovascular screening includes blood pressure measurement, lipid panel every 5 years starting at age 20, and diabetes screening with fasting glucose or HbA1c for at-risk adults. Adult immunizations include annual influenza vaccine, COVID-19 vaccines per current guidelines, pneumococcal vaccines (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23 for ages ≥65), shingles vaccine (Shingrix for ages ≥50), Tdap booster every 10 years, and HPV vaccine (ages 9-26, catch-up to 45). Lifestyle counseling on diet, physical activity, tobacco cessation, and alcohol moderation forms the foundation of preventive care.
